name: Next.js (App Router)
description: The dominant React meta-framework; file-based App Router with RSC and streaming

name.comment: Official name; the App Router is the current default routing model (introduced in Next.js 13, evaluated here at v16).

description.value: The dominant React meta-framework; file-based App Router with React Server Components, streaming, and server actions, built on React 19.2.
description.comment: Evaluated at Next.js 16.2.10 (July 2026); App Router uses React 19.2 canary features and Turbopack as the default bundler.

repository.comment: Official GitHub repository under the vercel org.

license.comment: SPDX MIT per GitHub license metadata and the npm `next` package manifest.

category.comment: Owns the build/server toolchain (Turbopack, server runtime); routing is one part of a full-stack framework, so per the guideline it gets the single meta-framework tag.

active-maintenance.comment: Repo pushed 2026-07-15; v16.2.10 released 2026-07-01 — very actively maintained by Vercel.

initial-release.comment: Repository created October 2016; Next.js 1.0 shipped that month. (The App Router itself arrived in Next.js 13, 2022, but the framework's initial release is 2016.)

latest-release.comment: next@16.2.10, published 2026-07-01; npm dist-tag `latest` also points to 16.2.10.

npm-weekly-downloads.comment: Weekly downloads of the `next` package for the week of 2026-07-08 to 2026-07-14.

routing-paradigm.comment: The App Router is purely file-system based: routes are defined by folders under app/ with page.tsx/layout.tsx conventions. No JSX <Route> tree or route-config array.

nested-layout-routes.comment: Nested layout.tsx files provide shared parent layouts that persist across child routes — a core App Router feature.

dynamic-params.comment: Dynamic segments via [id] folders (e.g. /details/[slotId]); params are read from the awaited params prop.

optional-catch-all-params.comment: Catch-all [...slug] and optional catch-all [[...slug]] segment folder conventions are supported.

search-params-api.comment: First-class access via the async searchParams prop in Server Components and the useSearchParams hook in Client Components.

programmatic-navigation.comment: useRouter().push/replace and redirect() provide imperative navigation beyond the <Link> component.

route-guards-middleware.comment: proxy.ts (formerly middleware.ts) intercepts matching requests for redirects/rewrites/auth guards before rendering; runs on every matching request including prefetches.

written-in-typescript.comment: Authored in TypeScript (with a Rust/Turbopack core) and ships first-class type definitions; TypeScript 5.1+ is required.

type-safe-paths-links.comment: typedRoutes (stable since 15.5, no longer experimental) generates route types in .next/types so <Link href> and router calls are checked against existing routes; non-literal hrefs need an `as Route` cast.

type-safe-params.comment: Global PageProps<"/details/[slotId]">/LayoutProps helper types (auto-generated by next typegen) infer params keys from the route path; values are still typed as strings, not domain types.

type-safe-search-params.comment: searchParams is typed as a generic { [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined } record; there is no built-in schema-based validation/typing of query params (unlike TanStack Router).

codegen-required.comment: Typed links and PageProps helpers rely on generated types in .next/types, produced during next dev/next build or on demand via `next typegen`; it is a generation step rather than pure inference.

type-safety-rating.comment: Typed links (typedRoutes) and route-aware params via PageProps, but search params are unvalidated and param values are plain strings — matches the guideline's rating 3 (typed params/links with gaps).

route-data-loaders.comment: Async Server Components fetch data on the server before render (the App Router's loader-equivalent); fetch requests are memoized/deduped within a render.

actions-mutations.comment: Server Actions ('use server') are a first-class write/mutation primitive tied to forms and route data, paired with cache APIs like updateTag().

prefetching.comment: <Link> prefetches route code/data on viewport/hover; Next.js 16 rewrote prefetching with layout deduplication and incremental prefetching.

pending-transition-ui.comment: loading.tsx (Suspense-backed) shows instant pending UI on navigation; integrates with React 19.2 transitions and View Transitions.

scroll-restoration.comment: The App Router restores and manages scroll position across navigations automatically (opt-in smooth scrolling via data-scroll-behavior in v16).

per-route-code-splitting.comment: Automatic per-route code splitting: each route segment is bundled and loaded independently.

per-route-error-handling.comment: error.tsx files define route-scoped error boundaries; during streaming the nearest error boundary catches errors and replaces only the affected section.

ssr-support.comment: Turnkey server-side rendering is the default; dynamic code runs at request time out of the box (opt into caching via Cache Components / use cache).

streaming-ssr.comment: Suspense boundaries and loading.tsx stream the static shell immediately and progressively send chunks via HTTP chunked transfer; each boundary streams independently.

react-server-components.comment: First-class RSC: App Router components are Server Components by default, with 'use client' marking client boundaries; the reference implementation of RSC in production.

static-site-generation.comment: Routes can be statically prerendered (generateStaticParams for dynamic segments, output: 'export' for a fully static build) and Partial Prerendering/Cache Components stream dynamic holes within a static shell.

client-only-usable.comment: Next.js is server-first: routes are rendered/prerendered through the Next build, not a drop-in client SPA router. output: 'export' yields a serverless static site (prerendered HTML), not a pure CSR-only router you add to an existing app.

framework-bound.comment: Requires the Next.js toolchain (Turbopack/webpack build + server runtime); the App Router cannot be used outside Next.js, so per the guideline it is framework-bound.

bundle-size.comment: Null per the guideline: Next.js is a meta-framework, so a single min+gzip 'router bundle' figure isn't meaningful or comparable to a standalone router package.

zero-runtime-dependencies.comment: The `next` package ships runtime dependencies including postcss, styled-jsx, @swc/helpers and caniuse-lite (plus optional platform SWC bindings and sharp).

tree-shakeable.comment: Automatic per-route code splitting and dead-code elimination via Turbopack (default) / webpack; unused server-only code is not shipped to the client.

documentation-quality.comment: Extensive docs: getting-started tutorials, App Router API reference, guides, migration/upgrade guides, interactive learn courses and codemods — matches rating 5.

official-devtools.comment: Next.js 16 ships a route-aware in-browser dev overlay plus Next.js DevTools MCP (Model Context Protocol) exposing routing/cache/render insight and unified logs.

data-fetching-integration.comment: Built-in server-side data fetching in Server Components (deduped fetch, use cache) is the primary mechanism, with RSC as first-class; docs also point to SWR/React Query for client-component fetching but those are optional, not built-in integrations.

predecessor-lineage.value: The App Router (Next.js 13, 2022) is the successor to the Pages Router; Next.js itself was created by Vercel (then Zeit) in 2016.
predecessor-lineage.comment: The App Router coexists with the older Pages Router but is the recommended, default routing model as of Next.js 13+.
